name: Yata Mirror
description: The Yata Mirror (八咫鏡, Yata no Kagami) is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing wisdom or honesty, depending on the interpretation. Forged by the smith Ishikoridome, it played a crucial role in luring Amaterasu, the sun goddess, out of the Ama-no-Iwato (Rock Cave of Heaven). When Amaterasu, distraught by her brother Susanoo's behavior, hid in the cave, plunging the world into darkness, the other gods devised a plan. They crafted the Yata Mirror and adorned a sakaki tree with it and other jewels. Ame-no-Uzume, the goddess of mirth, performed a provocative dance, causing the other gods to erupt in laughter. Drawn by the noise, Amaterasu peeked out of the cave, and upon seeing her reflection in the Yata Mirror, she was captivated by its brilliance and emerged, restoring light to the world. The Yata Mirror's reflective surface is believed to reveal the true nature of things, exposing lies and deception. It represents truth and sincerity, acting as a symbol of self-reflection and introspection. The mirror is currently enshrined in Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most sacred Shinto shrines in Japan, although it is never publicly displayed. Its presence is a constant reminder of the importance of honesty and wisdom in leadership and governance. It is an important symbol of the Japanese Imperial family and their connection to the divine.
subtitle: Symbol of wisdom and honesty.
material: Metal (believed to be bronze or copper)
origin: Forged by the smith Ishikoridome to lure Amaterasu from the cave.
currentLocation: Ise Grand Shrine, Japan (believed to be)

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu and the Cave
keyMyths.description: The Yata Mirror was used to lure Amaterasu out of the Ama-no-Iwato, restoring light to the world. The mirror's reflection captivated the sun goddess, drawing her out of hiding.

keyMyths.title: Imperial Regalia
keyMyths.description: The Yata Mirror is one of the Three Sacred Treasures, symbolizing the emperor's wisdom and connection to the divine. Its presence signifies the legitimacy and authority of the Imperial line.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
